refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows DPs to address enablement (e.g., responsive controls, efficient hydraulics, predictable motion) independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and representative site conditions, the NGHE solution's subsystems (propulsion, actuation, control response, load handling) do not introduce artificial delays or constraints that prevent the cycle from completing within CT_target. Measure end-to-end cycle time in controlled trials; confirm that any shortfall vs. CT_target is attributable to operator technique, site conditions, or load variability—not system design limitations.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall execute the load phase (bucket fill, material consolidation) without introducing artificial time delays.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall execute the travel phase (loaded and empty transit) without introducing artificial time delays.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall execute the dump phase (material discharge, bucket return to load position) without introducing artificial time delays.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall provide responsive control inputs (operator or tele-operator commands) with latency and fidelity sufficient to support cycle time execution.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independent sub-processes (load, travel, dump) with dedicated control logic and sequencing orchestration that enforces timing constraints at each stage.
DP2: Implement a unified state machine that models the entire cycle as a sequence of timed states with transition guards, enabling deterministic cycle time prediction and enforcement.

DP3: Allocate cycle time budget across load, travel, and dump phases; design each phase with parallel execution paths where feasible (e.g., load preparation during travel) to compress total cycle time.

DP4: Establish a real-time scheduler with priority-based task allocation that dynamically optimizes phase sequencing based on site conditions and workload variability, with fallback to nominal timing.

DP5: Decompose cycle execution into a primary control loop (cycle orchestration) and secondary feedback loops (phase-level timing correction) that continuously measure and adjust phase durations to meet p95 target.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Decompose cycle execution into a primary control loop (cycle orchestration) and secondary feedback loops (phase-level timing correction) that continuously measure and adjust phase durations to meet p95 target.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between phase-level feedback loops and primary orchestrator if correction logic is not cleanly separated (risk: cascading delays if one phase correction affects others).
Dependency on accurate phase duration measurement; if measurement instrumentation is unreliable, feedback loops become ineffective (risk: control instability).
Coupling between site condition variability and feedback loop tuning; aggressive correction may overshoot or oscillate under high variability (risk: p95 target miss).
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is selected because it balances determinism (primary loop ensures nominal cycle structure) with adaptability (secondary loops handle site variability). Coupling risks are manageable through clear separation of concerns: orchestrator owns cycle sequencing; phase controllers own timing correction within their domain. Measurement and feedback tuning are explicit dependencies that must be validated.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Phase duration measurement (load time, travel time, dump time) is available with sufficient accuracy (±5% or better) to enable meaningful feedback correction.
ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, load mass, operator skill) vary within a bounded range such that feedback loops can converge to p95 target without excessive oscillation.
ASSUMPTION: Cycle time target CT_target is achievable given physical constraints (e.g., hydraulic flow rates, travel distance, load mass); if not, no control strategy can meet it.
RISK: If measurement latency is high (>1 phase duration), feedback correction may lag and cause overshoot, missing p95 target.
RISK: If site conditions change rapidly (e.g., sudden terrain change mid-cycle), feedback loops may not adapt fast enough; primary orchestrator must have fallback nominal timing.

RISK: Operator variability (e.g., inconsistent load placement, travel speed) may introduce noise that feedback loops cannot filter; operator training and worksite procedures are critical dependencies.
RISK: p95 acceptance criterion requires statistical validation over many cycles; insufficient test data may lead to false confidence in meeting target.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Primary Orchestrator: owns cycle sequencing and nominal phase timing; enforces hard constraints (e.g., safety interlocks). (2) Phase Controllers: own phase-level feedback correction within bounded adjustment ranges (e.g., ±10% of nominal phase time). (3) Measurement & Telemetry: owns phase duration capture and statistical aggregation for p95 calculation. Add explicit assumption validation: confirm CT_target feasibility via physics-based cycle time model before committing to feedback-based design. Introduce a fallback mode: if feedback loops detect persistent inability to meet p95 (e.g., >3 consecutive cycles exceed target), trigger operator alert and revert to conservative nominal timing. Document measurement accuracy requirements and feedback loop tuning parameters as design inputs for DP→IM mapping.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized cycle orchestration engine with embedded feedback loop controllers for phase-level timing correction and p95 latency management

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Emit cycle execution metrics, phase duration measurements, and p95 latency observations for monitoring and analysis

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive cycle initiation requests and phase sequencing directives from higher-level task schedulers

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receive safety constraints that may interrupt or modify phase timing; emit alerts if timing corrections violate safety bounds

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicate with Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to coordinate distributed phase execution and collect real-time feedback on phase completion

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: cycle orchestration and feedback loop logic are core to NGHE's mission of coordinated execution. Primary control loop and secondary feedback loops are naturally housed here. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement phase-level sensors and actuators, but orchestration logic remains centralized.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a physical execution platform, not a control logic container. Placing orchestration here would scatter feedback loop logic across distributed agents, violating cohesion. Timing correction logic would diffuse to Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Weak containment: Teleoperation handles remote command and monitoring, not cycle-level orchestration. Feedback loops would require tight coupling to Vehicle state and Safety Services constraints, causing high diffusion.

Very poor containment: Safety Services enforces constraints, not orchestrates cycles. Placing primary control loop here violates separation of concerns. Timing correction logic would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (with Safety Services as a constraint provider via SafetyAlert interface)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) cycle orchestration is a core coordination function that NGHE is designed to provide; (2) feedback loops for phase-level timing correction are logically cohesive with cycle management; (3) centralizing this logic minimizes diffusion—Vehicle and Teleoperation remain execution agents that report phase completion and sensor data, while Safety Services provides constraint boundaries. The primary control loop and secondary feedback loops form a unified timing control subsystem within NGHE.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation agents operate with significant autonomy or latency, feedback from them may arrive too late for effective phase-level correction, requiring local timing loops at the edge. Mitigation: Define clear feedback latency budgets and consider hybrid architecture (centralized orchestration + local phase guards).

Risk: Safety Services constraints may conflict with p95 latency targets, forcing trade-offs. Mitigation: Establish safety-aware timing policies that adjust phase durations within safe bounds rather than violating safety for latency.

Risk: Network Link reliability affects feedback loop responsiveness. Mitigation: Design feedback loops with timeout and fallback mechanisms; ensure graceful degradation if network latency exceeds correction window.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP5 is well-aligned with NGHE Solution's role. No revision needed. However, consider refining DP5 to explicitly state: 'Decompose cycle execution into a primary control loop (cycle orchestration) hosted in NGHE Solution and secondary feedback loops (phase-level timing correction) that continuously measure phase completion via Network Link and adjust durations within Safety Services constraints to meet p95 target.' This clarifies the distributed nature of measurement (Vehicle/Teleoperation sensors) while keeping orchestration logic centralized.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses the FR by proposing a two-tier control architecture (primary orchestration + secondary feedback loops) designed to continuously measure and adjust phase durations to meet the p95 cycle time target. The logic is sound: decomposing cycle execution into orchestration and feedback correction is a valid design approach for meeting latency targets under variability. The DP is expressed in functional terms (what control structure to use) rather than implementation details, making it appropriately pitched for the design domain.

diffusion_risks: Network Link latency may exceed feedback correction window: If Vehicle or Teleoperation agents report phase completion with latency > correction budget, centralized feedback loops cannot adjust in time. Mitigation: Define explicit feedback latency SLAs (e.g., <100ms) and implement local phase guards at Vehicle/Teleoperation to enforce minimum phase durations independently.

Safety Services constraint conflicts: If SafetyAlert interrupts or modifies phase timing, the primary control loop must reconcile safety bounds with p95 targets. Risk of oscillation or timeout if safety constraints are too restrictive. Mitigation: Establish safety-aware timing policies that pre-compute feasible phase duration ranges within safety bounds.

Distributed sensor/actuator implementation: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement phase-level sensors and actuators (e.g., load sensors, travel distance sensors, dump completion detectors). If these implementations are inconsistent or delayed, feedback loop effectiveness degrades. Mitigation: Define sensor/actuator interface contracts and acceptance criteria for phase completion detection.

Network Link reliability: Feedback loops depend on continuous communication with Vehicle and Teleoperation. Network partitions or high jitter can break the feedback loop. Mitigation: Design timeout and fallback mechanisms; ensure graceful degradation to local phase timing if network latency exceeds threshold.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. This is excellent. The primary control loop (cycle orchestration) and secondary feedback loops (phase-level timing correction) are logically cohesive and best housed in NGHE because: (1) NGHE's core mission is coordinated cycle execution; (2) centralizing orchestration logic avoids scattering timing correction across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (3) Vehicle and Teleoperation remain execution agents that report state via Network Link, preserving separation of concerns. The minor diffusion (0.08) reflects necessary dependencies on Vehicle/Teleoperation sensors and Safety Services constraints, which are unavoidable and properly managed via external interfaces (Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert). No alternative module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) achieves better containment; all alternatives show poor cohesion and high diffusion (0.6–0.75). Recommendation: Accept NGHE Solution as the IM owner. Refine DP5 to explicitly state that orchestration logic is centralized in NGHE while measurement and actuation are distributed.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'Measured cycle time p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly verifiable via Telemetry interface. NGHE Solution can emit cycle execution metrics and p95 latency observations. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of phase duration measurements in NGHE Solution; (2) collection of cycle time samples under representative duty cycles; (3) statistical analysis to confirm p95 ≤ CT_target. The IM boundary clearly defines where measurement occurs (NGHE Solution receives phase completion feedback via Network Link and computes p95 latency). Feasibility is high if feedback latency budgets and network reliability are within acceptable bounds.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution with explicit responsibility for cycle orchestration and phase-level timing correction. External interfaces define how NGHE interacts with Vehicle (phase execution), Teleoperation (command/monitoring), Safety Services (constraint enforcement), and Tasking (cycle initiation). The allocation is unambiguous: NGHE owns the control logic; Vehicle/Teleoperation own execution; Safety Services owns constraint validation. However, allocation clarity could be strengthened by explicitly documenting: (1) feedback latency SLAs (e.g., Vehicle must report phase completion within X ms); (2) phase duration adjustment authority (can NGHE modify phase durations, or only recommend?); (3) fallback behavior if feedback loops fail (e.g., revert to fixed phase durations or abort cycle).

recommended_revisions: Refine DP5 to explicitly state distributed measurement and centralized orchestration: 'Decompose cycle execution into a primary control loop (cycle orchestration) hosted in NGHE Solution and secondary feedback loops (phase-level timing correction) that continuously measure phase completion via Network Link sensors (Vehicle/Teleoperation) and adjust phase durations within Safety Services constraints to meet p95 target.' This clarifies the DP<->IM bridge and reduces ambiguity about where measurement vs. orchestration occurs.

Add feedback latency budget to DP5: 'Secondary feedback loops shall operate with maximum feedback latency of [X] ms to enable phase-level correction within the p95 target window. If Network Link latency exceeds this budget, revert to local phase guards at Vehicle/Teleoperation.' This mitigates the network latency diffusion risk.

Define safety-aware timing policy: 'Phase duration adjustments shall remain within bounds enforced by Safety Services. If safety constraints conflict with p95 targets, prioritize safety and escalate conflict to task scheduler via Tasking interface.' This clarifies the SafetyAlert constraint interaction.

Document phase completion detection contract: 'Vehicle and Teleoperation shall implement phase-level sensors (load sensors, travel distance, dump completion) and report phase completion to NGHE Solution via Network Link with <[X] ms latency and >99% reliability. NGHE Solution shall validate phase completion signals and reject outliers.' This reduces diffusion risk from inconsistent sensor implementations.

Add fallback mechanism to DP5: 'If Network Link reliability drops below 99% or feedback latency exceeds budget, NGHE Solution shall activate local phase timing guards at Vehicle/Teleoperation to enforce minimum phase durations independently, ensuring safety and graceful degradation.' This addresses network reliability diffusion risk.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP5 logic is sound and the NGHE Solution IM is well-justified with 0.92 containment. However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal critical dependencies and constraints that are not yet decomposed into verifiable sub-requirements. Specifically: (1) feedback latency SLAs are mentioned but not formalized as FRs; (2) phase completion detection contracts are identified as diffusion risks but lack explicit functional requirements; (3) safety-aware timing policies and fallback mechanisms are design concerns that should be elevated to sub-FRs to ensure they are properly allocated and verified. Given high safety criticality, decomposing these concerns into next-level FRs is essential to manage risk and ensure traceability. Proceeding to sub-FR derivation will clarify ownership boundaries, latency budgets, and failure modes before detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Measure phase completion state from Vehicle and Teleoperation agents with feedback latency ≤ [X] ms and reliability ≥ 99% to enable timely phase-level timing correction within the p95 cycle time target.

FR5.2: Adjust phase durations dynamically within Safety Services constraints to correct deviations from p95 target, ensuring safety bounds are never violated and conflicts are escalated to task scheduler.

FR5.3: Detect and validate phase completion signals from distributed sensors (load sensors, travel distance, dump completion) to reject outliers and ensure consistent phase state representation across Vehicle and Teleoperation.

FR5.4: Implement local phase timing guards at Vehicle and Teleoperation agents to enforce minimum phase durations independently, enabling graceful degradation if Network Link feedback loops fail or exceed latency budget.

FR5.5: Monitor Network Link reliability and feedback latency in real-time; activate fallback to local phase guards if reliability drops below 99% or latency exceeds budget, ensuring continuous cycle execution under degraded conditions.

revise.why: DP5 should be refined to explicitly incorporate the five recommended revisions: (1) clarify distributed measurement vs. centralized orchestration; (2) add feedback latency budget constraint; (3) define safety-aware timing policy with conflict escalation; (4) specify phase completion detection contract with sensor/actuator interface requirements; (5) document fallback mechanism for network reliability degradation. These refinements will make DP5 more precise and reduce ambiguity about how secondary feedback loops interact with Safety Services and Network Link. The refined DP5 will serve as the parent for the next-level sub-DPs that address FR5.1–FR5.5.

assumptions: Feedback latency from Vehicle/Teleoperation to NGHE Solution can be bounded to ≤ [X] ms under normal network conditions (to be determined via network characterization study).
Phase completion detection can be reliably implemented via distributed sensors (load sensors, travel distance, dump completion detectors) with >99% accuracy and <[X] ms reporting latency.
Safety Services constraints can be pre-computed and expressed as feasible phase duration ranges, avoiding runtime conflicts with p95 targets.
Network Link reliability can be maintained at ≥99% under representative duty cycles and site conditions (to be validated via network reliability testing).
Local phase timing guards at Vehicle/Teleoperation can be implemented independently without requiring real-time synchronization with NGHE Solution.
open_risks: Network Link latency may exceed feedback correction window, preventing timely phase-level adjustment. Mitigation: Establish explicit feedback latency SLAs (FR5.1) and implement local phase guards (FR5.4) as fallback.

Safety Services constraints may conflict with p95 targets, causing oscillation or timeout. Mitigation: Define safety-aware timing policies (FR5.2) that pre-compute feasible phase duration ranges and escalate conflicts to task scheduler.

Distributed sensor implementations may be inconsistent or delayed, degrading feedback loop effectiveness. Mitigation: Define phase completion detection contracts (FR5.3) with acceptance criteria and interface specifications.
Network partitions or high jitter may break feedback loops. Mitigation: Design timeout and fallback mechanisms (FR5.5) with graceful degradation to local phase timing.

High safety criticality requires rigorous verification of phase timing correctness and safety constraint enforcement. Mitigation: Decompose into sub-FRs (FR5.1–FR5.5) to enable detailed verification and traceability.
evidence_needed: Network characterization study: Measure feedback latency distribution (mean, p95, p99) from Vehicle/Teleoperation to NGHE Solution under representative duty cycles and site conditions. Establish latency budget for FR5.1.
Sensor/actuator interface specification: Define phase completion detection contract (FR5.3) with sensor types, accuracy requirements, reporting latency, and outlier rejection criteria.

Safety-aware timing policy specification: Document feasible phase duration ranges for each phase under Safety Services constraints (FR5.2). Identify potential conflicts with p95 targets and define escalation procedures.
Network reliability testing: Validate Network Link reliability under degraded conditions (packet loss, jitter, latency spikes). Establish reliability threshold for fallback activation (FR5.5).
Local phase guard design: Specify minimum phase duration enforcement at Vehicle/Teleoperation (FR5.4) and synchronization mechanism with NGHE Solution orchestration.

Verification plan: Define acceptance criteria for each sub-FR (FR5.1–FR5.5) and traceability to p95 cycle time target. Include test scenarios for normal operation, network degradation, and safety constraint conflicts.
